student_message: I can't play with both hands at the same time
D1a_critique: Response is explicitly broken into four numbered methods (slow/steady, clap & count, walk & talk, encouragement), each with sub-steps.
D1b_critique: Ends with genuine student-facing questions: 'Which of these ideas sounds most appealing to you?' and 'Do you have a simple piece of music you're working on?'
D2_critique: The slow/steady section explicitly says to try one measure together and check comfort before adding more, and the closing asks the student to rate frustration to calibrate next steps.
D4_critique: Presents four separate practice methods simultaneously to a 7-year-old — this is three or more unrelated techniques at once, which overwhelms cognitive load.
D5_critique: Launches directly into solution methods without first asking what the student has already tried or how they currently practice.

student_message: Why do I have to practice scales? They're boring
D1a_critique: Breaks the response into four numbered sections: validate feelings, explain why, relate to interests, offer compromise — clear sequential decomposition.
D1b_critique: The response is entirely declarative instruction and suggestions; there is no genuine question directed at the student to guide their thinking toward understanding.
D2_critique: Teaches the value of scales and offers variation ideas but ends without asking the student to demonstrate understanding or try anything before moving on.
D4_critique: Covers four separate rationales for scales plus eight variation ideas in one response — multiple unrelated concepts across more than two categories.
D5_critique: Jumps directly into explaining why scales matter without first asking what the student already thinks or knows about scales.

student_message: I don't understand how to voice the melody over the accompaniment
D1a_critique: Explicitly broken into five numbered sections: what voicing is, why it matters, techniques, practical exercises, and applying to student's music.
D1b_critique: Closes with genuine student-facing questions: 'What piece are you working on right now?' and 'Can you describe a specific section where you're struggling?'
D2_critique: The 'Let's Talk About Your Music' section explicitly asks the student to describe their struggle and apply the techniques before advancing — verification through application.
D4_critique: Introduces dynamic contrast, touch/weight, tenuto duration, articulation, and pedal as separate techniques simultaneously — five distinct subtopics in one sitting.
D5_critique: Proceeds directly to explaining voicing without first asking what the student has tried or what they currently understand about dynamics or touch.

student_message: I learned the right hand but the left hand is different notes
D1a_critique: Three distinct sections with numbered sub-steps: why it's different, what to do (seven options), then diagnostic questions — clear decomposition.
D1b_critique: Asks 'What piece are you working on?' and 'What part of the left hand is feeling the most difficult?' — genuine questions directing student thinking.
D2_critique: Explicitly closes with 'Can you try playing just the left hand part of the piece slowly? I'm here to help if you get stuck!' — asks student to attempt before more instruction.
D4_critique: Presents seven simultaneous suggestions (LH only practice, slow/steady, clap rhythm, say notes, short sections, games, language metaphor) — far exceeds one main concept.
D5_critique: Goes straight to explaining why the left hand is different and listing solutions without asking what the student has already attempted.

student_message: My teacher said I need to use the pedal but it sounds muddy
D1a_critique: Four numbered sections — what the pedal does, why it's muddy, four exercises with clear sequences, and tips — all with sub-steps.
D1b_critique: Closes with 'Can you try that and tell me: How does it feel?' — a genuine question asking the student to try and report.
D2_critique: Explicitly says 'Let's start with Exercise 1. Can you try that and tell me how it feels?' — asks the student to attempt before moving on.
D4_critique: Covers what the pedal does, four separate causes of muddiness, four progressive exercises, and four separate tips — too many distinct concepts simultaneously.
D5_critique: Does not ask how the student is currently using the pedal or what they have tried; launches straight into explanation.

student_message: I can play Mary Had a Little Lamb! What should I learn next?
D1a_critique: Provides a tiered list of song recommendations organized by difficulty but does not decompose any task into sequential steps — it is a menu, not a method.
D1b_critique: Asks 'Which of those sounds the most fun to you?', 'Do you want to keep practicing the kind of notes...?', and 'Are you playing with just one hand or both?' — genuine student-facing questions.
D2_critique: Presents song options and closes with questions but does not ask the student to demonstrate or try anything before proceeding.
D4_critique: Focused on one topic — what song to learn next — and the options are organized by difficulty without introducing multiple unrelated concepts.
D5_critique: Launches into song recommendations before asking about the student's current skills or what they want to develop; the clarifying questions come after the suggestions.

student_message: My arm gets tired holding the bow
D1a_critique: Three clearly labeled sections — What's Happening, What We Can Do Together, What You Can Do — with multiple sub-steps in each, forming a logical sequence.
D1b_critique: Asks 'Can you tell me when your arm gets the most tired?' and 'Does it hurt or just feel tired?' — genuine diagnostic questions directed at the student.
D2_critique: 'Let's try a quick shoulder roll right now!' explicitly asks the student to perform an action in the moment, which is a real-time check before continuing.
D4_critique: Covers four separate causes of tiredness, five tutor-led interventions, and five home exercises simultaneously — well beyond one focused concept.
D5_critique: Does not ask how the student currently holds the bow or what they have already tried; proceeds directly to listing causes.

student_message: How do I do vibrato?
D1a_critique: Four sequentially numbered exercises building from isolated wrist movement to combined wrist/forearm to eventually adding the bow — clear progression.
D1b_critique: The visible response is entirely declarative instruction; no genuine question is directed at the student to guide their thinking or invite reflection.
D2_critique: Presents four exercises with no instruction to try one and report back before moving to the next; there is no comprehension check between steps.
D4_critique: Covers what vibrato is, where movement originates (wrist, forearm, finger), and four separate exercises simultaneously — multiple distinct concepts for one session.
D5_critique: Does not ask whether the student has attempted vibrato before or what their left hand position currently looks like; jumps straight to instruction.

student_message: I can play the notes but my teacher says it doesn't sound musical
D1a_critique: Organized into three major categories — Tone Production, Rhythm & Phrasing, Expression — each with sub-techniques and exercises, forming a clear breakdown.
D1b_critique: The entire visible response is declarative explanation and exercise lists; no question is directed at the student to scaffold their own thinking.
D2_critique: Delivers content across three sections with no instruction to try something and report back before advancing to the next concept.
D4_critique: Three major topic categories each with multiple sub-techniques — tone, phrasing, expression — are presented simultaneously, far exceeding one focused concept.
D5_critique: Does not ask what specifically the teacher said or what the student has already tried to make the music sound more musical before launching into instruction.

student_message: What does forte mean?
D1a_critique: Provides a direct vocabulary definition plus a contrast with piano — this is a one-block explanation, not a decomposition into sequential steps.
D1b_critique: Asks 'Can you play a short phrase, first piano and then forte?' and 'Do you want to try that now?' — genuine questions inviting the student to engage.
D2_critique: 'Can you play a short phrase, first piano and then forte?' explicitly asks the student to demonstrate the concept before moving on.
D4_critique: Focused on one concept: the definition of forte and how to apply it in playing — clean and appropriately narrow.
D5_critique: Immediately provides the definition of forte without first asking whether the student has any guess or prior exposure to the term.

student_message: The strings hurt my fingers
D1a_critique: Three labeled sections — Why it Hurts, What to Do Right Now, What to Do Long-Term — plus a technique check, forming a coherent sequential breakdown.
D1b_critique: Asks 'Can you show me how you're holding down the strings?', 'What kind of guitar do you have?', and 'How long have you been playing?' — genuine student-facing questions.
D2_critique: 'Can you show me how you're holding down the strings with your fingers? I want to see...' explicitly asks the student to demonstrate technique before more instruction.
D4_critique: Covers the science of calluses, four immediate relief strategies, string gauge, guitar setup/action, and technique check simultaneously — multiple unrelated categories.
D5_critique: Does not ask how long the student has been playing or what they have tried before launching into explanation of why fingers hurt.

student_message: I can play chords but switching between them is slow
D1a_critique: Three categories — Understanding, Exercises (five distinct methods), Mindset — with numbered sub-exercises, forming a structured breakdown.
D1b_critique: 'Let me know what those are if you want specific advice' is a conditional invitation, not a genuine guiding question directed at the student to scaffold thinking.
D2_critique: Delivers all content including exercises and then ends with 'Have Fun!' with no request for the student to try something and report back.
D4_critique: Covers five distinct exercise types plus mindset/practice habits — clearly exceeds one focused concept and would require multiple sessions.
D5_critique: Does not ask which specific chord changes are slow or what the student has already tried; proceeds directly to comprehensive instruction.

student_message: I want to improvise over a blues progression but I don't know where to start
D1a_critique: Five numbered sections building sequentially from understanding the progression through scales, connecting to chords, blues note, and exercises.
D1b_critique: The visible response is entirely declarative instruction with no genuine question directed at the student to guide their thinking.
D2_critique: Delivers all five sections of content with no check asking the student to try something and report back before moving to the next concept.
D4_critique: Covers the 12-bar progression, minor and major pentatonic scales, connecting scales to chord tones, and the blues note as four separate concepts in one response.
D5_critique: Does not ask what the student already knows about scales, theory, or blues before launching into comprehensive instruction.

student_message: I learned the pentatonic scale but I sound the same every time
D1a_critique: Three main categories — Understanding, Variation (four sub-areas), Application — with lettered exercises under each, forming a structured breakdown.
D1b_critique: No genuine question is directed at the student within the visible response to guide their thinking; instruction is purely declarative.
D2_critique: Delivers all content and cuts off without asking the student to try anything and report back.
D4_critique: Covers different pentatonic positions, target notes, rhythmic variation, and bending/vibrato as four separate sub-areas simultaneously.
D5_critique: Does not ask what positions or approaches the student has already tried before explaining the problem and solutions.

student_message: My voice cracks when I sing high notes
D1a_critique: Three sections — what happens physiologically, why at this age, and what to do — with named exercises under the third section forming a sequential breakdown.
D1b_critique: Closes with three genuine diagnostic questions: 'What song are you singing when you notice the cracking?', 'What note specifically cracks?', and 'How does it feel?'
D2_critique: The closing questions explicitly ask the student to reflect on and describe their specific experience before more targeted instruction can be given.
D4_critique: Covers the physiology of voice cracks, age-related context, three warm-up exercises, breath support (two techniques), vowel shapes, and a 'don't push' warning simultaneously.
D5_critique: Starts with physiological explanation rather than first asking what the student has already tried or what they notice when their voice cracks.

student_message: How do I sing with more emotion?
D1a_critique: Three numbered sections — understanding/connecting, technical aspects, exercises — with sub-points forming a clear sequential breakdown.
D1b_critique: 'Please let me know which area you'd like to focus on first' is a meta-preference prompt, not a question that guides the student's thinking about the content.
D2_critique: Delivers three content sections and cuts off without asking the student to attempt anything and report back.
D4_critique: Covers emotional connection, vocal dynamics, tone color/timbre, phrasing/rubato, and resonance as distinct areas — five separate topics in one response.
D5_critique: Does not ask what the student currently does when trying to add emotion or what they have already attempted; launches into content immediately.

student_message: I don't want to practice
D1a_critique: Four sequential steps: validate feelings, understand why, address the underlying issue (branching by cause), and offer alternatives — clear process decomposition.
D1b_critique: Multiple genuine questions within the response: 'Can you tell me a little bit about why you don't want to practice right now?', 'Is there something about practicing you don't like?', 'Are you tired?'
D2_critique: This is a motivational conversation guide with no content taught, so there is no comprehension check — but per definition, no verification before advancing occurs.
D4_critique: Focused on one problem: why the student doesn't want to practice and how to address the underlying cause — coherent and not overloaded.
D5_critique: 'Can you tell me a little bit about why you don't want to practice right now?' explicitly probes the student's situation BEFORE providing any solutions.

student_message: Can you just tell me the answer?
D1a_critique: No task is decomposed into steps — the response asks three clarifying questions to identify what the student needs, which is appropriate but not step sequencing.
D1b_critique: 'Can you tell me a little bit about what you're struggling with right now?', 'What have you already tried?', and 'What part is confusing you most?' are all genuine guiding questions.
D2_critique: No content is taught in this response, so no comprehension check is possible or present.
D4_critique: Narrowly focused on one purpose: redirect the student toward collaborative problem-solving rather than just giving the answer — not overloaded.
D5_critique: 'What have you already tried?' is a direct prior knowledge probe asked before any instruction is given.

student_message: I don't know
D1a_critique: No task is decomposed into steps — the response offers clarifying options to understand what 'I don't know' refers to, which is not step sequencing.
D1b_critique: 'What were we just talking about?', 'What feels tricky?', and 'Do you just need a little reminder?' are all genuine questions guiding the student to articulate the problem.
D2_critique: No content is taught in this response, so no comprehension check is applicable.
D4_critique: Entirely focused on one goal: understanding what the student doesn't know before doing anything else — appropriately narrow.
D5_critique: The entire response is prior knowledge probing — 'What were we doing when you said I don't know?' is asked before any teaching content is provided.

student_message: I practiced for an hour but I'm not getting better
D1a_critique: Three labeled categories — what you're practicing, how you're practicing, other factors — each broken into sub-questions and points forming a diagnostic framework.
D1b_critique: Two explicit mid-response 'Let's pause' sections with genuine student-facing questions: 'What pieces are you currently working on?', 'Do you feel like those pieces are challenging?'
D2_critique: Two explicit 'Let's pause here' checkpoints with questions directed at the student before advancing to the next section — genuine mid-teaching verification.
D4_critique: Covers what you're practicing, five sub-points on how you're practicing, and sleep/food/stress/enjoyment — three separate unrelated categories.
D5_critique: Begins with 'here's a breakdown of things we can look at' before the student has described their practice — launches into a diagnostic framework rather than asking first.

student_message: I can't play with both hands at the same time
D1a_critique: Clear sequential steps: practice each hand separately, then a bulleted sequence (RH on lap, LH plays 3x, switch, then both together slowly), then build measure by measure.
D1b_critique: 'Would you like to try playing just two notes together first — one with each hand? We can make it into a game!' is a genuine question inviting student engagement.
D2_critique: 'Would you like to try playing just two notes together first?' asks the student to attempt something specific before more instruction, functioning as a comprehension check through action.
D4_critique: Focused on one strategy — how to combine hands gradually using isolation and chunking — all steps serve the same single goal.
D5_critique: Jumps directly to the solution method without first asking what the student has already tried or how they currently practice.

student_message: Why do I have to practice scales? They're boring
D1a_critique: Provides two rationales and a list of fun variations but does not decompose any task into sequential steps — it is a persuasion and suggestion response, not a step breakdown.
D1b_critique: 'What's a song you really want to learn? I bet we can find scales hiding in it!' is a genuine question directing the student toward discovering the relevance themselves.
D2_critique: Explains benefits, offers fun variations, and ends with a question about a song preference — no request for the student to demonstrate understanding before moving on.
D4_critique: Focused on one topic — why scales matter and how to make them less boring — the two rationales and suggestions are tightly connected to the same question.
D5_critique: Launches directly into explaining scale benefits without first asking what the student already thinks or knows about why they play scales.

student_message: I don't understand how to voice the melody over the accompaniment
D1a_critique: Four numbered practical steps: use different arm weight, heavy thumb exercise (with sub-instructions), think in layers, physical tips — clear sequential decomposition.
D1b_critique: 'What piece are you working on? I can give you specific tips for that music!' is a genuine question directing student toward applying the concept.
D2_critique: 'Play a simple piece you know well, but exaggerate the melody...Then gradually find the right balance' asks the student to attempt the technique and self-assess before advancing.
D4_critique: All four steps address one concept — voicing the melody — with the arm weight, exercise, layers metaphor, and physical tips all serving the same focused goal.
D5_critique: Immediately begins explaining voicing without asking what the student has tried or what they currently understand about dynamics between hands.

student_message: I learned the right hand but the left hand is different notes
D1a_critique: Four numbered steps: practice LH alone, Superman method (with sub-steps), go super slow with both hands, practice just 2 measures — clear sequential breakdown.
D1b_critique: 'What piece are you working on? Maybe I can give you specific tips for that song!' is a genuine question directed at the student.
D2_critique: The closing question asks about the piece but does not ask the student to try a specific step and report back — it is diagnostic, not a comprehension check of what was taught.
D4_critique: All four steps address one problem — combining two different hand parts — with each step being a variant of the same chunking/isolation strategy.
D5_critique: Does not ask what the student has tried when attempting the left hand before offering the tips.

student_message: My teacher said I need to use the pedal but it sounds muddy
D1a_critique: Numbered four-step pedal exercise sequence (play, press after, lift before next note, play new note then press again) plus organized sections — clear step decomposition.
D1b_critique: 'Would you like to try the pedal exercise with a specific part of your piece?' is a genuine question inviting student action.
D2_critique: 'Would you like to try the pedal exercise with a specific part of your piece?' asks the student to attempt the technique before receiving more instruction.
D4_critique: Focused on one concept: how to execute pedal changes cleanly — the numbered exercise and 'start small' sections all address the same narrow technique.
D5_critique: Does not ask how the student is currently using the pedal or what they have tried before explaining the pedal change technique.

student_message: I can play Mary Had a Little Lamb! What should I learn next?
D1a_critique: Offers two tiers of song recommendations with a bonus tip about dynamics — this is a menu of options, not a decomposition of a task into sequential steps.
D1b_critique: 'Which of these sounds fun to you? Or is there another song you've been wanting to learn?' is a genuine question guiding the student toward expressing their preference.
D2_critique: Presents song options and a dynamics tip but does not ask the student to try anything or demonstrate understanding before concluding.
D4_critique: Focused on one topic — what to learn next — with song options organized by challenge level; the dynamics tip is a bonus, not a separate major concept.
D5_critique: Lists song suggestions before asking about the student's current skills, what they're ready for, or what they want to develop next.

student_message: My arm gets tired holding the bow
D1a_critique: Three sections — quick fixes, bow hold check (with three specific points), and fun exercises (three named activities) — forming a logical sequential breakdown.
D1b_critique: 'Would you like to try one of these exercises together? Or show me how you're holding your bow so I can help?' is a genuine question inviting student demonstration.
D2_critique: 'Show me how you're holding your bow so I can help' explicitly asks the student to demonstrate before more corrective instruction is given.
D4_critique: All three sections address one problem — tired arm from bow holding — with the fixes, hold check, and exercises all targeting the same issue.
D5_critique: Does not ask how the student is currently holding the bow before explaining the quick fixes and bow hold adjustments.

student_message: How do I do vibrato?
D1a_critique: Three numbered exercises building progressively: polishing exercise (hand rocking), wave motion (away from violin), then start slowly on instrument — clear sequential progression.
D1b_critique: 'Would you like me to explain any of these steps more?' is a genuine question checking whether the student needs clarification.
D2_critique: 'Would you like me to explain any of these steps more?' explicitly checks understanding before assuming the student can proceed independently.
D4_critique: Focused on one skill — beginning vibrato — with three exercises all targeting the same wrist-rocking motion at progressive levels of difficulty.
D5_critique: The 'first make sure you're ready' prerequisite check lists conditions but does not ask the student directly whether they have tried vibrato or what their hand position feels like.

student_message: I can play the notes but my teacher says it doesn't sound musical
D1a_critique: The exercise has three numbered steps (play as happy story, secret, angry/frustrated), plus the 'start with these basics' section provides a structured breakdown.
D1b_critique: 'Would you like to tell me about a specific piece you're working on?' and 'What emotion or story does this piece convey?' are genuine questions guiding student reflection.
D2_critique: The three-way exercise explicitly asks the student to perform and self-observe ('Notice how your bow, dynamics, and timing naturally change?'), functioning as active comprehension check.
D4_critique: Dynamics, phrasing, and bow control are introduced as tightly unified aspects of musical expression — all serving one concept — not three separate lessons.
D5_critique: Does not ask what the student has tried to make the music more expressive or what specifically the teacher pointed to before providing instruction.

student_message: What does forte mean?
D1a_critique: Provides a direct definition with an analogy — this is a one-block explanation with no decomposition into sequential steps.
D1b_critique: 'Would you like to try playing something forte right now?' is a genuine question inviting the student to engage immediately.
D2_critique: 'Would you like to try playing something forte right now? We can practice making your violin sing with a strong, confident voice!' asks the student to demonstrate before concluding.
D4_critique: Focused on one concept — what forte means and how to apply it — clean and appropriately narrow.
D5_critique: Immediately defines forte without first asking whether the student has any guess or prior exposure to the term or dynamic concepts.

student_message: The strings hurt my fingers
D1a_critique: Three sections — quick fixes (three bullets), callus development explanation, guitar setup check — form a sequential triage from immediate to long-term.
D1b_critique: 'Would you like to learn some easier songs that use fewer string presses while your fingers get stronger?' is a genuine question offering student agency.
D2_critique: The closing question offers a pivot to easier songs but does not ask the student to try something and report back — it is a preference offer, not a comprehension check.
D4_critique: All three sections address one problem — finger pain from strings — with each addressing the same issue from a different angle (immediate, developmental, equipment).
D5_critique: Does not ask how long the student has been playing or what they have already tried to manage finger pain before providing advice.

student_message: I can play chords but switching between them is slow
D1a_critique: Five clearly numbered strategies: two chords only, anchor finger trick, freeze and release, chord shadows, and metronome — clear sequential options.
D1b_critique: 'Which chord changes are giving you the most trouble? I can give you specific tips for those!' is a genuine question targeting the student's specific problem.
D2_critique: Provides five strategies and then asks a diagnostic question at the end — this is not a comprehension check asking the student to try something before moving on.
D4_critique: Five distinct techniques (anchor finger, freeze and release, chord shadows, metronome, plus the basic two-chord drill) exceed one focused concept for a single session.
D5_critique: Does not ask which chords the student is switching between or what they have tried before listing five strategies.

student_message: I want to improvise over a blues progression but I don't know where to start
D1a_critique: Clear sequential sections: blues scale, 12-bar pattern, start simple (three numbered steps), box 1 pattern, essential techniques — progressive structure.
D1b_critique: 'Would you like me to show you the fingering pattern for that first blues box, or do you want to know more about phrasing?' is a genuine question directing student choice.
D2_critique: Delivers all instruction and offers a fork-in-the-road question at the end — this is a preference question, not a comprehension check asking student to try and report.
D4_critique: Blues scale, 12-bar chord progression, box 1 fingering pattern, and bending/vibrato/hammer-ons are four distinct concepts presented in one response.
D5_critique: Does not ask what the student knows about scales, chord progressions, or blues before launching into comprehensive instruction.

student_message: I learned the pentatonic scale but I sound the same every time
D1a_critique: Three sections — rhythm tricks, note choices, flavor techniques — each with specific action-oriented sub-bullets forming a structured breakdown.
D1b_critique: 'What style of music are you most interested in playing?' is a genuine question directing the student to provide information that guides next steps.
D2_critique: Delivers all content and asks a style preference question at the end — not a check asking the student to try something and report before advancing.
D4_critique: Rhythm variation, note choice strategies, and four separate flavor techniques (bending, vibrato, hammer-ons, slides) are presented as distinct areas — exceeds one focused concept.
D5_critique: Does not ask what the student has tried to vary their playing or what positions/techniques they already use before listing solutions.

student_message: My voice cracks when I sing high notes
D1a_critique: Five numbered tips — warm up, think 'float', relax shoulders, yawn feeling, practice 'oo' sounds — form a clear sequential breakdown of approach.
D1b_critique: 'Would you like to try a fun exercise together to help with those high notes?' is a genuine question inviting the student to engage.
D2_critique: 'Would you like to try a fun exercise together to help with those high notes?' asks the student to attempt something before more instruction proceeds.
D4_critique: All five tips address one problem — voice cracking on high notes — with each being a specific angle on the same technique adjustment (support, relax, placement).
D5_critique: Does not ask when or how the voice cracks, or what the student has already tried, before providing the five tips.

student_message: How do I sing with more emotion?
D1a_critique: Four sections — start with lyrics, connect to experiences, use body, technical tips — with sub-bullets under each forming a clear breakdown.
D1b_critique: 'What song are you working on right now? I'd love to help you find specific emotional moments to focus on!' is a genuine question directing student toward application.
D2_critique: The practice exercise asks the student to 'pick one line and sing it five different ways' — this is an active comprehension check requiring the student to attempt before receiving more guidance.
D4_critique: Covers lyric analysis, personal emotional connection, body language, and four separate technical tips — four distinct categories presented simultaneously.
D5_critique: Does not ask what the student currently does when trying to add emotion or what they have attempted before listing the four approaches.

student_message: I don't want to practice
D1a_critique: Offers four fun alternatives (game, timer, stickers, favorite song) as parallel options — these are choices, not sequential steps in a process.
D1b_critique: 'Can you tell me: What's the hardest part about practicing?' with multiple choice options is a genuine question guiding the student to articulate their resistance.
D2_critique: Presents alternatives and closes with a question — no content is taught and no comprehension check is performed.
D4_critique: Focused on one problem — getting a 5-year-old to want to practice — the four suggestions all address the same motivation barrier.
D5_critique: 'Can you tell me: What's the hardest part about practicing?' explicitly asks the student to identify the barrier BEFORE committing to any specific solution approach.

student_message: Can you just tell me the answer?
D1a_critique: No task is decomposed into steps — the response explains why it won't give the answer and asks what the student needs, which is appropriate redirection but not step sequencing.
D1b_critique: 'What specific part are you having trouble with?' and 'Can you tell me what you're working on?' are genuine questions guiding the student toward collaborative problem-solving.
D2_critique: No content is taught, so no comprehension check is possible or present.
D4_critique: Narrowly focused on redirecting toward collaborative learning — not overloaded with concepts.
D5_critique: 'What specific part are you having trouble with?' probes what the student knows and has tried before any instruction is given.

student_message: I don't know
D1a_critique: No task is decomposed into steps — the response asks clarifying questions to determine what 'I don't know' refers to, which is appropriate but not step sequencing.
D1b_critique: 'Can you help me understand what you're not sure about?' with specific option bullets is a genuine question guiding the student to articulate the problem.
D2_critique: No content is taught in this response, so no comprehension check is applicable.
D4_critique: Focused on one goal — understanding what the student doesn't know — appropriately narrow and not overloaded.
D5_critique: The entire response is prior knowledge probing; 'Can you help me understand what you're not sure about?' is asked before any teaching content is offered.

student_message: I practiced for an hour but I'm not getting better
D1a_critique: Four numbered steps in the practice method: pick 4-8 measures, play super slowly, fix mistakes right away, only speed up when perfect 3 times — clear sequential breakdown.
D1b_critique: Three guiding questions asked before the instruction block: 'Are you practicing the whole piece or small sections?', 'When you make a mistake, what do you do?', 'Are you playing at full speed?' — genuine and scaffolding.
D2_critique: 'What piece are you working on? I'd love to help you make a specific practice plan!' invites the student to engage and apply the advice to their actual situation.
D4_critique: Focused on one concept — deliberate chunked practice — the four steps are tightly unified around the same core strategy.
D5_critique: The sentence 'how we practice matters just as much as how long' is delivered as a teaching point before the diagnostic questions, meaning instruction begins before probing is complete.
